subject: [PATCH v2 18/18] Documentation: iomap: update docs to reflect iomap_next model

2026-07-01.patch_summary: This patch updates the iomap documentation to reflect a change in how filesystems interact with the iomap_next() callback. Instead of having separate callbacks for beginning and ending an operation, every operation now takes a single iomap_next() callback that finishes the previous mapping (if any) and produces the next one. The update also clarifies how filesystems typically implement this by using the iomap_process() helper to build the iomap_next() callback from begin and end helpers.

reviews.author: Darrick Wong
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong questioned the assumption that filesystems will always use iomap_process(), asking for clarification on when they might not

Reviewer Darrick Wong pointed out that the documentation for iomap_end should be updated to reflect that it must persist data for write operations, specifically mentioning that iomap_writepages does not handle inline data.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation for iomap_next() does not accurately describe its behavior, specifically that it is responsible for finishing the previous mapping in addition to producing the next one.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested adding a transition sentence to clarify that filesystems should call iomap_process instead of implementing all behaviors in ->iomap_next themselves, and also proposed demoting the next section for better clarity.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation update looks good and is fine as it is, indicating no issues or requested changes.
reviews.raw_body:
I wonder, under what circumstances would a filesystem /not/ use
iomap_process()?

---

Unrelated to this patch, but this should say "For write operations, the
end function must persist the data" because iomap_writepages doesn't
handle inline data.

---

Each call?  Oh, each implementation of ->iomap_next must finish the
previous mapping.

---

s/and/or/

I think there should be a transition sentence here along the lines of

"Most filesystems are not expected to implement all of these behaviors
in ->iomap_next themselves.  They should instead call iomap_process as
described below."

Or demote the next section so it's more obvious that the "iomap_process"
and "->iomap_next" sections aren't independent?

---

<nod> Looks fine.

--D

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledges that iomap_next() callback may require filesystem-specific state, but does not indicate a plan to address this concern in the current patch series.

Author agreed to incorporate reviewer's suggested changes into the patch
reviews.raw_body:
Hmm, maybe a situation where they need to share or carry some
filesystem-specific state / info between finishing the mapping and
producing the next one?

---

Sounds good, I'll incorporate all the suggestions you recommended.

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Darrick Wong
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the iomap_next model does not allow for the ->begin method to set the private field and the ->end method to dispose of it, as the next ->begin would pass a new mapping to the next ->begin. He suggested making iomap_iter_continue preserve the iomap/srcmap private pointer across memset(0) calls or changing the signature of iomap_process to avoid passing iomap/srcmap explicitly.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the documentation still uses '->iomap_begin' and '->iomap_end' callbacks in some places, which should be updated to use 'iomap_next' instead.
reviews.raw_body:
The ->begin method can still set iomap::private and the ->end method can
dispose of it, right?  Oh, wait, no, that doesn't work because you're
talking about ->begin/->end passing something to the next ->begin.

I suppose you could make iomap_iter_continue preserve the iomap/srcmap
private pointer across the memset(0) calls.  But all current users
either rely on private being zeroed by iomap_iter after ->iomap_end
returns, so that would be quite the change.

Hm.  I was thinking that the signature for iomap_process could be
cleaner if you didn't have to pass iomap/srcmap explicitly.
iomap_process could do the (dangerous) casting from the (const struct
iomap_iter *) to the (struct iomap *) pointers before calling ->begin
and ->end.

But if you do have an ->iomap_next function that doesn't use
iomap_process, then it has to do the pointer extraction itself.  We
already expose the innards of struct iomap_iter to callers, so maybe
it's ok to have some gross helpers like:

static inline struct iomap *iomap_iter_iomap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
{
	return (struct iomap *)&i->iomap;
}

static inline struct iomap *iomap_iter_srcmap(const struct iomap_iter *i)
{
	return (struct iomap *)&i->srcmap;
}

I'm not sure *that*'s any cleaner.  And I think it gets confusing with
the other srcmap extraction function.

Eh never mind, I've talked myself out of this. ;)

---

<nod>

--D

2026-07-02.patch_summary: This patch updates the iomap documentation to reflect the new iomap_next model, where every iomap operation takes a single iomap_next callback directly. The callback finishes the previous mapping and produces the next one. Most filesystems build it from begin and end helpers via the iomap_process helper. This change replaces the separate ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks used in older versions of iomap.

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Christoph Hellwig suggested moving ->private from struct iomap to struct iomap_iter to address constness and per-operation concerns, and also recommended switching the low-level begin/end ops to pass the iter instead of using container_of.
reviews.raw_body:
Should we move ->private from struct iomap to struct iomap_iter?
That'll deal with the constness and the fact that private data
really is a per-operation thing.

That also reminds me that now that we actually still keep the low-level
begin/end ops we need to switch them to a calling convention that
passes the iter instead of the ugly container_of.  This is something
I wanted deferred until we get the iter conversion, but it turns out
that now leaves them untouched..

reviews.author: Darrick Wong
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that he had previously used the iomap.private field to store per-mapping private data during the fuse-iomap development work, but this approach is no longer relevant and does not constitute a strong argument against the current design.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested changing the signature of the iomap_begin_fn callback to take a struct iomap_iter pointer instead of passing parts of it as separate arguments.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested simplifying the call sites of the iomap_next callback by passing a single const struct iomap_iter pointer instead of three separate arguments, and explicitly casting to remove constness from the two iomappings.
reviews.raw_body:
I dunno -- towards the end of the fuse-iomap development work I actually
had started using iomap.private to store per-mapping private data.  But
that work is dead now, so that's not a strong argument.

---

Oh, you mean changing the signature to:

typedef int (iomap_begin_fn)(struct iomap_iter *iter...);

instead of passing parts of the iter as separate arguments?
Yeah, that would be nice.

---

Let me try again.  Instead of passing three arguments:

int fubar_iomap_next(const struct iomap_iter *iter,
	struct iomap *i, struct iomap *s)
{
	...
}

pass one instead:

int fubar_iomap_next(const struct iomap_iter *iter)
{
	struct iomap *i = (struct iomap *)&iter->iomap;
	struct iomap *s = (struct iomap *)&iter->srcmap;
	...
}

to simplify the call sites.  The gross part of this is (1) having a
const iomap_iter pointer to prevent filesystems from screwing around
with the iter, and therefore (2) the need for explicit casting to remove
the constness from the two iomappings.

--D

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
Author responded to a question about whether struct iomap_iter already has a private field for caller-supplied context, explaining that users of iomap->private (gfs2, ntfs3, fuse-dax) do use it for per-mapping data and release it in the same iteration.

Author acknowledges reviewer's point about including changes in the same series, but asks for clarification on whether to include them or make a separate follow-up series.

Author agreed to address a concern about the documentation update, specifically the description of iomap_next() and its interaction with ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end().
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not sure I understand. Doesn't struct iomap_iter already have a
private field that holds caller-supplied per-operation context?
afaics, the users of iomap->private (gfs2, ntfs3, fuse-dax) do use it
for per-mapping data and release it in the same iteration.

---

That's a good point. Do you think it'd be better to include those
changes as part of this series or do that as a separate follow-up
series that targets the same merge window timeline as this one?

---

Agreed.

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Darrick Wong
reviews.summary: Reviewer Darrick Wong pointed out that the documentation does not clearly distinguish between per-file-operation data and per-mapping data passed to iomap callbacks, but then dismissed his own concern as 'nonsense'.
reviews.raw_body:
Oh right, iomap_iter::private is a private pointer that the filesystem
can sometimes set to pass per-file-operation data to ->iomap_{begin,end}
... that is tortuously extracted from the iomap_iter via the iomap
pointer.

iomap::private is a private pointer that the filesystem can set to pass
per-mapping data from ->iomap_begin to ->iomap_end.

IOWS, never mind, I'm babbling nonsense.

--D

2026-07-03.patch_summary: This patch updates the iomap documentation to reflect the new iomap_next model, where every iomap operation takes a single iomap_next callback directly. The callback finishes the previous mapping and produces the next one. Most filesystems build it from begin and end helpers via the iomap_process helper. This change replaces the separate ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks used in older versions of iomap.
